video_title: How Ukraine Put Russia on the Backfoot
transcript: You are listening to the War on the Rocks podcast on strategy, defense, and foreign affairs. [music] My name is Ryan Evans and I'm the founder of War on the Rocks. In this episode, I speak with Mike Kaufman and Daram Masico, both of the Carnegie Endowment on the Ukrainian battlefield. Specifically, we dig into how and why things are looking much better for Ukraine, [music] or at least better than they have been for a while. This was recorded right before the bruhaha over Zilinsky firing, a very popular defense minister. But the good news is we have some free content on that at war onthe rocks.com. It's a free edition of the Ukraine Compass, one of our membersonly newsletters, which I think you'll enjoy. This episode is brought to you by our friends at Booze Allen, an advanced technology company that builds commercialgrade products and solutions for America's most critical defense, civil, and national security priorities. They're also doing some exciting work in Europe. And I speak with Joe Bice, one of that company's most talented executives on exactly that in a short conversation in the middle of this episode. You can learn more at wartherocks.com/boozealen. That's B O O Z A L L E N. >> How would you describe the phase of the war that we're at? >> I think you always debate whether or not this is an inflection point or a true turning point. To me, we are seeing a turning point. Ukraine is in the best position it's been in the war since summer of 2023 >> before their offensive. >> Yes. Yes. Before the failed summer 2023 offensive. The question is, has it established a lasting advantage or is this a temporary one creating a window of opportunity? It's clear that the Russian offensive has floundered for a whole host of reasons. We've discussed them before on the podcast. Russians have had consecutive months of essentially making no gains or actually losing territorial control. Ukraine has the initiative due to its successful campaign targeting Russian logistics operational depth isolating Crimea and Russia's focus primarily on the bombardment campaign in some ways to compensate for the lack of progress of their forces on the ground. The big question is is Ukraine setting the conditions for some kind of limited offensive operations? Is it simply trying to get leverage for negotiations that they're hoping to have ahead of the winter? because they too are quite vulnerable as you see Ukraine as largely out of uh ammunition for western provided missile defense and when winter comes there's a concern that the Russian hand might strengthen once again. >> Could you talk about the sources of some of the Ukrainian operational success and momentum that we're seeing now? >> Yeah, I think if we look at the Crimea operation in particular, I think we can say that there's been a sequencing and real thought to this campaign over the past six plus months. So there has been an attempt to first downgrade Russian air defenses in occupied Ukraine near the land uh land bridge to Crimea. We are seeing a sustained priority against targeting logistics along that land route. Russia has had to compensate by moving counter drone teams like Rubicon and other elements like that from other parts of the front to cover down on that which opens up opportunities for Ukraine elsewhere. We are also seeing a strike campaign on the electrical grid in Crimea. We are now seeing Russians trying to leave because there are gas shortages, food shortages, and people are really worried. I think we can say that Ukraine is really imposing different kinds of dilemmas on Moscow, mostly through the drone and and growing intermediate strike campaigns that we're seeing. Crimea, homeland Russia, all of that. >> This campaign against Russian energy infrastructure has been particularly impressive in Crimea and in, you know, actual Russia proper. And some of this relates to what we discussed, Mike, in terms of Ukraine filling the donut hole, you know, before they had this, you know, short-range drone capabilities, FPVs on and around the front. And then this long range stuff. A lot of this energy infrastructure is not very far from the Ukrainian border that they've been striking. Some of it is further in long range, but it's pretty remarkable what they've been able to accomplish. >> Yeah, I think that part of the campaign is essentially the cumulative effect. They've been after Russian energy infrastructure and fuel refining capacity for 2 years now. summer months you get peak usage in Russia and they have pretty significant constraints in supply and this year Ukraine has been able to generate a genuine fuel crisis what they've been trying to do for the last 2 years because they finally have the number of drones they have the sorty rates they have the range to hit most of the refineries for several thousand km and they've done enough cumulative damage to create a lot of strain on the system does this change at all the calculus as far as we can tell in Moscow we've seen some weird theater from Putin in the last few days, the last couple weeks, you know, he did this staged visit to the front that clearly looked like it was a sound stage. It was kind of embarrassing the poor production value that went into that. I feel like I could stage a better fake visit to the front. >> It was poor value. Deeply disappointed. >> Yeah, I could make a better fake command post at the front. >> Zero stars awarded. >> Next Russia could finish the episode with fake command post. >> Yes. And then you had this weird, you know, Putin saying that they've seized this town and Zilinsky being like, "Actually, you didn't." So, it does seem like the Kremlin is struggling to find some sort of we're still winning narrative, but does this actually change the political calculus? >> I like to look for little quirks in the system when they're feeling strain and I'm seeing them. So, if you flash back to the battle of Avdfka, I think that was that was a year year and a half ago. Things weren't going well on the Russian front at that point and the only thing that they could really speak to was this small town of Avdka. And I remember that Putin was getting his briefings from his seniors and it was a blown up map of Abdiva and that was the only village that was on this map and they were like look at all this progress we've made and we're really we're really charging through and we're talking at you know 500 meters basically and we're seeing that again but now it's just a it's a different name. What does it matter? How do these strikes put political pressure on Putin? I think the strike on Moscow, if we're just talking about pure like political embarrassment, probably Moscow, because it is the most heavily defended city on Earth. I think we can make that claim. Between air defenses and drone defenses, it is very heavily defended and a lot of Ukrainian drones got through and I think that's probably speaking to the sorty rate. They're Russians are intercepting them. Ukraine is putting a lot against this issue and I think we see the Russians typically retaliate against that. We see these strikes against Keev that are moving away from purely defensive targets, although they're still hitting defense industry targets and now they're hitting in the city center with back to hitting apartment blocks, right? >> And and you think about it in the sense of well, you know, I don't necessarily have the technical data to know what is an intercept that then crashed. Was it a deliberate hit on this suburb block? Was it jammed? I don't have that technical data, but I do know that whenever they do this, they tend to play it on Russian state media because they have to have something to show their population because they're embarrassed, right? Like Moscow fuel tank blows up in this catastrophic way and now we're going to show images of us striking back on these cities. That's the kind of behavior that we've seen when there's stalls on the ground. We're seeing it again. >> Yeah. Yeah, I guess what's different about this phase and as Mike you you just you said Ukraine's been working at this for a couple years, so it's not a totally new thing, but what what is newer is the effects of the war being extremely visible and visceral to ethnic Russians in Moscow and and St. Petersburg, which the Russian state made enormous effort to insulate ethnic Russians in the western part of Russia from the direct effects of the war. people were living their lives as normal and buying luxury goods and you know that were smuggled in from China and now it's different now that people don't have fuel. They don't know when a drone's going to hit something. For a long time Russians have basically been able to largely ignore the war. If you wanted to fight in this war you could take a contract and sign up and if you didn't you didn't have to. After the initial partial mobilization wave pretty much everyone else was contracted and most Russians could largely go on with their lives kind of generally annoyed but ignoring the war. When Ukraine has started to bring the war to Russia, the challenge politically is that Putin doesn't really care much of what average Russians think. If he did, he would have never started this war and we wouldn't be in it 5 years in. He doesn't seem to care nearly as much what the Russian elite thinks either because they too have paid a significant price for this war and most of them I don't think very much supported. Although they've adapted to making money one way or another off of defense spending and sort of have realigned their revenue streams over the course of this war. So the big question is will will this really affect political calculus. My own view is that the real challenges for a long time here I agree with Dar Putin's been fed the sense by the Russian military that even though they're going much slower than they would like their success is inevitable. They will eventually take deta and it's clear to me that not only is objectively not going to happen but it will probably take real reversals on the ground to change those impressions. The kind of things that the Russian military can't hide by producing bogus maps. And then the costs are stacking. They are they are real. I mean, you see members of the technocratic elite, the head of the central bank, Napoleon, and others, >> who still seems to be disappeared, last I read, or she reappeared in public. Yeah. >> No, she she's around, but I think she's actually eventually going to retire. And she's been a big part of what's kept that system going to some respects. I think you can hear him clearly sounding the alarm that this all of defense spending is not sustainable and is structurally imbalancing for the economy. like they are beginning to increasingly publicly talk about the issue of sustainability. >> And you know, this is something that Lorie Friedman and I spoke about in a previous episode is that Putin actually doesn't have a lot of alternative sources of information. He gets briefed what he gets briefed and we actually don't know how much he knows about what's really going on. Like this is a guy that as far as we know may have never used the internet before in his life. Like we just don't know what his other access to information is. How are the tactics on the ground evolving? I think we can say that the methods that the Russians have created are not really evolving that much in the last year. The infiltration tactics, they've got them to a point and then that's where they are. And I just really haven't seen them introduce some new element into that process to change things. And the Ukrainians have figured out what they're going to do. They're going to use motorcycles, ground assaults, drones, waves of drones. I mean, it's it's a composite force. But again, there's not really a lot of new thought there. It's a very complicated battlefield and I think that we're seeing the limits of Russia not really being able to come up with an operational or excuse me, a tactical to solution to this problem. And so it's just the same idea applied 12 months. >> Mike, you and I, especially earlier in the war, talked about how Ukraine innovates faster, but Russia innovates better at scale. So once they adopt an innovation, they're better at fielding it across the entire force. And you can go back and listen to older episodes about why that is. But what is stalling in the Russian system of innovation because I think she made a great point about that there it just doesn't seem to be existing anymore on the Russian side. The way I characterize it is on the one hand you did see last year Russia innovate considerably in drone employment. the expansion of Rubiconly drone formations, the experimentation with their own version of line of drones and in a tug of war over who has advantage superiority in the sort of drone established kill zone. They did substantially degrade Ukraine's advantage. The problem they had is that the rest of the force couldn't take advantage of that or do anything with it for a very particular reason. They had made a conscious decision to focus on fighting through a perpetually regenerated regenerating assault layer within the force. Essentially grind their way through the front. And that meant that they spent years focusing on conducting smallcale combat operations across a very broad front instead of discrete offensives. You know, the kind of discreet offensive operations that we're used to in a conventional war. The advantage is that they just could could sustain that and keep it going for a very long time. and they thought that eventually it would lead to a collapse of Ukrainian defense. The disadvantage is that's very hard to achieve a breakthrough fighting that way. And what I think happened to them last year is that they were steadily gaining more territory. On paper, if I showed you the numbers, they gained more territory in 2025 than 2024. And that they got fundamental, >> it's a low bar, but yeah, >> right. But they are fundamentally lazy believing that yes, even though they are taking higher losses, they're fighting less and less efficiently that eventually they are going to reach their objectives and they don't need to substantially change what they're doing. They can just keep fighting in this way. And Ukraine just kept optimizing and adapting for the way they were attacking. So when we got to 2026, the Russian offensive late March starts to open up again the exact same way they started things back in 2025. Not much changed about Russian ground operations. quite a few things was changing in the Ukraine side because Ukraine actually spent 2025 finally reforming and going after big problems they've had in the force since 2022. Things that we've talked about in this podcast for a long time. Things that have been acknowledged throughout the Ukrainian armed force ministry of defense. And they actually spent 2025 trying to address a number of these issues. Stabilizing the manpower situation. Taking the brigades and putting them into course. Experimenting with ways to conduct localized counterattacks with drone enabled formations. All these things essentially have combined. Whereas the Russian problems grew, Ukraine started actually addressing some of the issues they've had for a while. >> I have u one thing to add on to that. I think the new wave of technology that we were seeing the early signs of being implemented on the battlefield at scale last year was supposed to be Russian combat robotics. There were all these things in the pipeline because they had studied and observed what Ukraine was putting out in the field and they were testing and they were getting ready to go and gear up and introduce these things large scale. Two things happened. Russians got cut off from Starlink and a lot of those combat robotics that they were testing were built using covert or clandestine black Starlink. So none of them worked anymore after they got cut off Starlink. Number two, there are multiple competing entities in Russia who want to be the lead for this and there are a few corruption scandals in some of these combat robotic firms. So I think that was a technology that was supposed to be introduced to help implement some changes on the ground and then it it failed for internal um reasons. We are going to take a quick break from our conversation with Mike Andera to talk to my friend Joe Bice who's a senior vice president at Booze Allen Hamilton and he's working on some pretty interesting stuff as far as European security is concerned. I started by asking him what exactly [music] his job involves. We're one of the few companies that takes a holistic view of this space. In fact, I have to give credit to Herasio Rosanski, our CEO, who said to me 6 years ago, "You don't need to convince me that security cooperation is important. What you need to tell me is what we're going to do about it and how we can help the US government." It's a little bit of everything. If you look at the US's position on allies and partners, you hear a lot about America first does not mean America alone. And if you don't believe that, you can look no further than the national defense strategy that was released where allies and partners were mentioned over 45 times. Look at the two executive orders on foreign military sales that are going to drive uh efficiency, transparency, and really just speed of getting this equipment and major platforms to allies and partners. We do have a global outlook on this and we're focused on Europe because of all the security risks that are occurring right now in that part of the world. We've actually been working with the Air Force and developing something called tactical operations light or TAL and it's built from our modular detachment solutions kit. And what it really is is a command and control solution that allows for war fighters to both connect sensors, decision makers, and weapons all the way to the edge. [music] And right now, we're deploying that in support of the US Air Force across Europe. And we see great applicability for that same product with allies and partners globally. You can learn more about Boo Allen Hamilton's exciting work in Europe by going to warns.com/booen b o z [music] a len. Now back to my conversation with Mike and Dara. Do we see any changes on Russian manpower efforts? >> There's two things. I think we can probably be reasonably confident that Russia's been missing their recruiting targets for maybe 2 or 3 months at this point. They can recover from that. they've recovered from that kind of you know problem before. Um we just have to look at it in in longer periods of time than month. If they miss it for four 6 months then I start to wonder okay what is this going to mean on the ground. But the bigger issue is that we are and we have been seeing this is a steady force degradation over time just in terms of the quality of people that they're getting in and also the quality of the officers that are coming into the force. Right? So at the junior level like your lieutenants and your captains are being either pulled from reserve and they're like 40 50 they haven't served in 20 plus years or there are cadetses with very condensed education to try to get them to the field as fast as possible. So we're seeing that force decline. We're seeing a lot of violence really become system systematic at this point. And I think that's having some effect. I wouldn't say the majority effect and Mike and I talk about this a lot but it is a factor just the the decline in discipline really across the front. >> We've seen a lot of speculation recently some in the commentary but also some from senior officials and NATO member states that Russia may seek to in response to its degrading position in the war escalate either in Ukraine or or somewhere against a NATO member state. What do you make of this talk? Where do you think it's coming from? Some of it seems like it might be based on intelligence based on who's saying it, but it's very hard for me to judge. >> We've talked about this a lot. I've read all those articles and I'm reading between the lines in them. And I I I do think that some more specificity is needed in in this conversation. There's different ways that Russia can escalate. And I think we should talk about what we can feel confident in versus what is seems to be pure speculation at this point. So, will Russia escalate against Ukraine if they're facing embarrassing setbacks at home and a setback on front? Yes, we've seen them do that many times um in the course of this war. I think this summer we should anticipate Russia testing and probing to see if the Patriot interceptor depletion is true or not. I don't think they're going to believe that at face value. They're going to test it to see. So, I do think unfortunately what that means is more ballistic missile strikes, damaging ones against Keefe in particular. So yes, I do think we can anticipate escalation against Ukraine. I do think we can anticipate some sort of escalation against NATO member states in the sense of sabotage operations. Those have been ongoing consistently for the past four years and majority of them are intercepted. I do think that we will see that we'll see them getting sportier and testier over time. This is a process that's been ongoing for 4 years. What I don't think has had enough specificity in some of the conversations about Russian escalation right now is it seems to almost be vague like they're going to escalate militarily against NATO. And I find that impossible. Not impossible, but not credible. >> Not credible because with what like with what Russian forces are you going to do this? They are like the land power is occupied right now in Ukraine like the air force is no. No. So I just I think that there's >> soft rates maybe. >> But for to what end? I mean if you're this is the question that no one can answer for me is if Putin feels like he's losing catastrophic control, why would he attack NATO visibly? You would lose all control over the next steps. [laughter] >> I agree. It's it'd be kind of crazy and I'm just playing devil's advocate here. But one thing that Putin is exceptionally good at is misreading the psychology of Western leaders. He's made so many mistakes because of that. I agree, but I I think there's still a distinction between leaving your calling card with assassinations or arson, you know, like this spy versus spy stuff, which he's good at and likes doing, versus a suicide mission, which is an overt military attack on NATO. I just think there needs to be a little bit more specificity in here. And the the thing I would caution against because this this this happens. I find these escalation fears reemerging pretty consistently whenever Russia's doing bad on the front line. Let them do bad guys. Like we don't need to panic. We don't need to panic. >> Losing in our psychology, collective psychology don't understand. >> And I would say we do not need to panic on behalf of the Kremlin. Just let them feel. >> I think I'm largely with Dar on this. I don't think Russia has great escalation options against Ukraine to be honest. It has very few against NATO members. There's the unconventional campaign that they've been prosecuting throughout Europe which has not gotten them much and actually gets detected and Jerry is not nearly that good at it. I think we know quite a bit about it. There's a potential for you know more drone sorties and penetrations of NATO member airspace like what happened with Poland. You can see that. But what are they going to get out of at the end of the day other than annoying the alliance and leading to more and more initiatives to essentially counter the Russian drone threat which in some ways actually very positive for NATO to get after this problem. Russia has really no prospects for a military operation against NATO and even if the war was over would take quite some time for Russia to reconstitute sufficiently to pose a major threat in in that way. So I see a lot of this is unrealistic. It's interesting that some of these escalation fears spread now. You could get them a bit maybe in 2022 when Russia was really losing. There was a bit of a nuclear crisis if you recall fall 2022 >> which Colin call just wrote a great piece on that. I I forget what publication but I highly recommend if you search for Colin Call nuclear crisis with Russia you'll find it. >> But that's not the case now. Like yes we have a turning point in the war but it's not the same situation was in 2022 where people thought that you know victory military victory was at hand. It might even be a shorter war. At least that was the sense in Ukraine back in 2022. Russian forces are not doing well. Time is clean on Russia's side, but it's also not losing in the same way it was it was seemed to be losing back in 2022. So, there's really no from my point of view, there isn't a strong basis for these fears, which I mean, it could be way off, but >> yeah. And as before with the nuclear crisis, like what would tactical nuclear use get Russia on the battlefield? They'd radiate an area that they would then seize that they want to be a part of Russia. >> I'm circling back to the comment I made earlier. you know, this is there's a tendency to perceive fear on behalf of the Kremlin and just don't do that. And I still think the 22 crisis was a misinterpretation of multiple things that led people to a conclusion that Russia was considering nuclear weapons. And I I get it. >> It was taken very seriously. >> It was taken right and you have to take it you have to take it seriously, but I would say that there's a duty to warn, but there is also a duty to provide reasoned judgment along with it. and and I don't I don't think that that happened in in 22 um just just based on what I know of that time period. And so anyway, I I just I would not want us to get into that loop again where it looks like Russia is stalling on the ground and Putin is getting ki. We don't need to perceive escalation fears on behalf of the Kremlin. He'll create his own reality. >> Putin does have a pretty easy way out now at this point of war, which is just agree to a ceasefire. Yeah, >> the western position, the Ukrainian position has narrowed so much that the demand is not even really an unconditional ceasefire. It could even be a conditional ceasefire where Russia gets probably >> an Iran style ceasefire where there's no actual ceasing of the firing. Yeah. >> Yeah. Where [laughter] we're we're not sure. But um but the bottom line is it's not 2022 or 2023. Like if Putin wanted to, he could agree a ceasefire tomorrow and say, "Yeah, actually this is what we're wanting to take." If you think there's anybody in Russia looking closely in the map and says, you know, actually the point of this war is to get from Constantin to DKIka and then from Duska to chromatsk because that's where all the action really is. And until we get chromatsk and savans, two cities I'm familiar with cuz I've been them probably, you know, several times a year. Until we get these cities, you know, we just can't we just can't consider the largest conventional war in Europe since World War II to be successful. Who are the groups in Russia right now where if a ceasefire along the line of contact was declared, who would oppose that? Would it be the people? No. They are checked out of this war, the elites, no. They're happy for a relief. Like the only >> even the right-wing Russian telegram crowd would probably be for it. >> They are increasingly asking the question like why are our tactics so bad? Like what is the plan? So look, those guys are all controlled anyway. They're going to get their new talking points from Belusv and they'll be fine >> or they won't be fine. They'll be in prison. It's a personal authoritarian regime. It's not There's no Shampoon. Staying up at night worrying about what Russian bloggers think, I think is a strong misreading of how authoritarian regimes. >> It's very it's very, you know, it's something we're familiar with is in Washington, a lot of our friends worry about what the administration will think about XYZ when the administration isn't paying attention to them at all. >> Well, just think of it just think of it this way, okay? So, you know, the Russian like mill blogger base, it's like 50 or 60 mostly guys, a few women. They bring them in to the Ministry of Defense. Do you know what that means? That means they've got their phone numbers. It's got they've got their phones tapped. They know where they live. Like if there's any problems this they you know what I mean? Like there's not like who these bloggers are going to lead the charge against the Kremlin like in the Posian rebellion. No, this matters to Putin and the band of weirdos around him in the echo chamber. And he is perceiving his wishes as the wishes of the state because for him they're the same thing. I am the state. Mike, you sent me a piece by Zulusni that was in the Telegraph yesterday. Just to remind the audience, Zelusni was the lead commander of Ukrainian forces until he was ousted by Zalinsky and made the ambassador to the UK. But he issued what I think it's fair to describe as sort of a balancing corrective saying that yes, Ukraine is doing better, but let's stop this talk that Ukraine is inevitably going to win and that Russia is about to lose and let's let's be a little Is that a fair characterization? >> It is. He issued a sobering corrective that I think overall was a decent depiction of where we are in the war right now. Granted, he has his own political motivations because he's seen as the likely contender in any election to run against Zilliansky and he naturally doesn't want it to be seen that Zilliansky essentially won or solved the war. So I want to, you know, I want to contextualize the writing in this way. But nonetheless, his overall assessment was Ukraine is doing well, but position on the ground, it's still closer to something like a stalemate than not, and that we have to be careful with freighting this moment with expectations. I do appreciate that and I think I have a sense of why he's writing it because as commander-in-chief, he himself was the victim deacto of overly frighted expectations for the summer 2023 offensive based on how well they did in fall 2022. And given what he had to work with that operation, I mean, you could say that we, you know, I've criticized it up and down. I I was there during that summer. Other people have too. And Zelusian himself was pretty frank, I think, about some things that went wrong. But the bottom line is I think he has a good point based on what happened in the first year and a half of the war to be careful not to replay this movie in 2026. Yeah. Yeah, I mean absent a collapse of Russian forces, it's very hard to see how Ukraine musters enough manpower and material for a real offensive against Russia to seize back territory. They just don't have enough people still. They can't make all of it robots. Even though the advances they've made with not just aerial drones, but ground drones has been obviously impressive, but it the offensive potential of the Ukrainian military just doesn't seem like it's there to me. Ukraine will likely try something, but it's important to separate a successful localized offensive somewhere with an operation that can achieve something close to decisive military victory or fundamentally changes the dynamic in this war. I personally do think that the one thing that can change Putin's mind is essentially a reversal that the Russian military can't hide, right? a loss a loss of currently occupied territory such that the Russian military can only put up an inaccurate map and say we are you know 30 kilometers forward of where we actually are because it's across every TV station and there's no way to hide this but not just a few hundred meters something >> right but that said I think Zillusion's warning is is useful here not to take a force that is in many ways exhausted after years of fighting and has done a great job on the defense but nonetheless it's not the force that Ukraine was fighting with in20 22. It's not the force Ukraine had for the offensive in 2023 and then freight it with expectations of decisive military victory at this point in this war. Right? And not to confuse what is a good turning point for victories around the corner because there's always been those voices that keep saying that 3 months from now we'll be in Crimea. Some of them are Western commentators too by the most of them. I think >> I would like to point out that the Ukrainians when they are not feeling that pressure from Western partners to do something, they actually come up with they come up with really good operations and they understand their opponent. And I agree with Mike right now. They're putting multiple dilemmas on the table, but they're not being pressured to do this offensive and absorb that risk. Yeah, as the kids say, let them cook. >> I think that's a great place to end the episode. Thank you both so much for joining me and uh we'll have you back on in I believe late July or early August again. Right. >> Sounds good. >> Yeah. Thanks for having us back. [music] >> Thanks. >> Thanks for listening to this episode of the War in the Rocks podcast. Check out our membership program at war ontherocks.com/membership. And thanks so much to our friends at Booze Allen. Learn more about the important work that they're doing at war ontherocks.com/booen. [music] It's B O Z A L L E [music] N.

>> How would you describe the phase of the
war that we're at?

this is an inflection point or a true

position it's been in the war since

>> Yes. Yes. Before the failed summer 2023
offensive. The question is, has it
established a lasting advantage or is
this a temporary one creating a window
of opportunity? It's clear that the
Russian offensive has floundered for a
whole host of reasons. We've discussed
them before on the podcast. Russians
have had consecutive months of
essentially making no gains or actually
losing territorial control. Ukraine has
the initiative due to its successful

operational depth isolating Crimea and
Russia's focus primarily on the
bombardment campaign in some ways to
compensate for the lack of progress of
their forces on the ground. The big
question is is Ukraine setting the
conditions for some kind of limited
offensive operations? Is it simply
trying to get leverage for negotiations
